place: Brookside CDP, New Jersey, New Jersey

facts.text: Brookside Historic District earned its spot on the National Register of Historic Places on February 16 1996, celebrated for its architecture, industry, and community life from 1780 to 1942.
Since 1923, Brookside’s Fourth of July Parade starts at 9 a.m. on Dogwood Drive and ends at the Community Club Field with food, games, and family fun.
The Whippany River, an east‑flowing tributary of the Passaic, cuts through Brookside’s center and is joined by Harmony Brook coming from the north.
Early European settlers dubbed the valley Waterville—or Water Street—because its abundant water powered numerous mills.
Brookside Historic District contains 114 contributing buildings, six structures, and eight sites that together tell the town’s long story.
Brookside sits roughly six miles west of Morristown, the county seat of Morris County.
